args:
A 43-year-old woman, with postliver transplant (2008) secondary to primary sclerosing cholangitis-related cirrhosis, maintained on tacrolimus, presented to our hospital with 4-day history of sore throat and fever and 2-day history of skin rash. On examination, patient was found to be febrile with temperature of 38.2 celsius, B.P 100/80 mmHg, respiratory rate (R.R) 24/min, and heart rate (H.R.) 130/min. Oxygen saturation on room air was 85%. Systemic examination revealed congested eyes, pharyngeal erythema, maculopapular skin rash involving face, upper limbs, and trunk. Chest examination revealed decreased breath sounds on the right basal area and crackles on the left side posteriorly till the mid-zone. Basic laboratory work-up was as follows: WBC: 9.8 × 103/μL, neutrophil: 7.7 × 103/UL, lymphocyte: 0.9 × 103/UL, BUN:2.80 mmol/L, creatinine: 88 μmol/L, Na: 129 mmol/L, K: 4 mmol/L, Cl: 99 mmol/L, HCO3: 18 mmol/L mmol/L, bilirubin T: 7.6 μmol/L, albumin: 28 gm/L, ALT: 50 U/L, AST: 84 U/L, ALP: 268 U/L, INR: O.9, CRP: 53, ESR: 29 and tacrolimus level: 3.4. Hepatic transaminases showed progressive increase over 2 days. Ultrasound of abdomen and liver was normal. A CT pulmonary angiogram was ordered due to the presence of tachypnoea, tachycardia and drop in oxygen saturation. It did not show any evidence of PE but showed bilateral basal lung infiltrates more on the right side with associated two large size basal lung cysts (Fig. ) .
Patient was admitted as a case of viral exanthemata fever and bronchopneumonia with airborne precautions and was treated with intravenous antibiotics and fluids. The respiratory viral panel came negative. Viral serology results were positive for CMV Ab IgG, EBV nuclear antigen IgG, EBV capsid antigen IgG, rubella IgG Ab, measles IgG Ab, and measles IgM Ab. Autoimmune serologies and tuberculosis work-up were negative. On day 2 of admission, patient's skin rash started to fade from the face and patient showed clinical improvement with regard to fever and constitutional symptoms. Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage to evaluate lung infiltrates and cystic lung disease showed negative work-up for acid-fast bacilli, Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia, and fungi. Measles virus PCR in serum was reported positive. She was managed with fluids and supportive measures. The patient improved clinically with the fading of skin rash gradually and normalization of liver enzymes over the next 5 days and was discharged home.

What is the patient's score of Wells' criteria for Pulmonary Embolism? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

The patient had a heart rate of 130/min, which is >100 bpm (1.5 points). There is no mention of DVT symptoms, hemoptysis, prior DVT/PE, recent immobilization/surgery, or active malignancy in the note. Therefore, based on the documented findings at admission, only the tachycardia criterion is clearly met.

Final score: 1.5.

args: A 63-year-old woman arrived to the emergency evaluation unit late this afternoon complaining that her right lower extremity had become “thick from hip to toes” over the past thirty-six hours, making it difficult for her to slip into her usual walking leggings and causing a heavy dragging sensation whenever she climbed stairs. She recalls finishing a community gardening event two evenings ago without incident; when she awoke the next morning, the entire limb looked puffier than the left, a discrepancy that progressed steadily through the day despite alternating periods of sitting, standing, and short walks around her house. She denies twisting injuries, falls, insect bites, animal scratches, recent rashes, or localized skin infections and has noticed no fever, night sweats, chills, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, dysuria, or systemic malaise. There has been no shortness of breath, pleuritic chest discomfort, hemoptysis, palpitations, or presyncope. Three years ago she completed six months of rivaroxaban for a sonographically confirmed popliteal and posterior tibial vein thrombosis in the same extremity that had developed after a transatlantic flight; she has taken no anticoagulant since and her subsequent annual physicals have been unremarkable. Her history is otherwise notable only for well-controlled hypertension and hypothyroidism; she takes lisinopril 10 mg daily and levothyroxine 75 µg daily, supplemented by over-the-counter vitamin D, and she specifically denies estrogen replacement, selective estrogen-receptor modulators, glucocorticoids, erythropoietin, or androgen therapy. She has never been diagnosed with malignant disease, inflammatory bowel pathology, nephrotic syndrome, or congestive heart failure. She underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy eight years ago and an uncomplicated colonoscopy fifteen months ago, but no operative or invasive procedures since. She walks her dog twice daily for twenty minutes, performs light resistance training twice a week, has no residual neurologic weakness, and has never worn a lower-limb cast, boot, or splint. She quit cigarette smoking fifteen years ago after a 10-pack-year exposure, drinks a glass of red wine at family dinners once or twice monthly, and denies recreational drug use. A paternal uncle experienced a pulmonary embolism in the setting of prolonged immobilization after hip fracture, but no first-degree relative has had venous thromboembolism, early myocardial infarction, or stroke. On arrival—before any medication was given—she was seated upright, conversing in full sentences, and shifting her right foot periodically to ease the sense of fullness. Pre-treatment vital signs were: temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 128/76 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats/min and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air. Cardiac examination revealed a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear throughout. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender with no hepatosplenomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities showed conspicuous enlargement of the entire right limb from the inguinal ligament to the dorsum of the foot compared with the left, with skin that appeared mildly taut yet maintained normal pink coloration and temperature comparable to the contralateral side. A flexible tape measure placed 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity recorded a circumference of 39.4 cm on the symptomatic side and 35.1 cm on the left, establishing a difference of 4.3 cm; 15 cm proximal to the superior patellar border measured 58 cm on the right versus 53 cm on the left. Gentle thumb pressure held for five seconds over the anterior tibial crest and over the dorsum of the foot produced instantaneous recoil without residual indentation, mirroring the contralateral limb, indicating an absence of pitting. Careful palpation along the femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, posterior calf compartment, and distal posterior tibial track revealed diffuse fullness without discrete cords or exaggerated tenderness, and ankle dorsiflexion against resistance did not provoke sharp pain. Passive and active ranges of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle were complete and painless. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2 + and symmetric, capillary refill of the toes was under two seconds, and motor strength as well as light-touch sensation remained intact throughout both legs. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, no knee effusion, no palpable Baker cyst, no brawny hyperpigmentation, and no serpiginous superficial venous channels coursing along the thigh or calf. A point-of-care urinalysis was unremarkable. Baseline laboratory samples drawn prior to any antithrombotic therapy revealed hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, platelet count 276 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 7.1 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, serum creatinine 0.88 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 20 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 18 U/L, international normalized ratio 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 29 seconds. A resting electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm at 86 beats/min with no acute ischemic changes. Plain anteroposterior and lateral radiographs of the femur and tibia obtained by the referring urgent care clinic earlier today demonstrated only diffuse soft-tissue prominence without fracture, calcification, or gas. No cellulitic erythema, ruptured tendon, joint effusion, lymphatic obstruction, or lymphedema was evident on inspection or palpation, and the prior history of venous thrombosis together with the new circumferential enlargement that encompasses the full length of the limb raises strong suspicion for recurrence; therefore, comprehensive duplex ultrasonography of the right iliac, femoral, popliteal, and calf venous segments has been ordered emergently while the patient remains fasting and under monitoring.

What is the patient's score of Wells' criteria for Deep Vein Thrombosis? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

No cellulitic erythema, ruptured tendon, joint effusion, lymphatic obstruction, or lymphedema was evident on inspection or palpation... There is an alternative diagnosis for the symptoms (not mentioned)? (The physician notes: \"no cellulitic erythema, ruptured tendon, joint effusion, lymphatic obstruction, or lymphedema was evident\")", "required_values": ["age", "sex", "history_of_DVT", "active_cancer", "paralysis_paresis_or_recent_cast", "recently_bedridden_or_surgery", "localized_tenderness_along_deep_venous_system", "entire_leg_swollen", "calf_swelling_greater_than_3cm_compared_to_asymptomatic_side", "pitting_edema_confined_to_symptomatic_leg", "collateral_superficial_veins", "alternative_diagnosis_at_least_as_likely_as_DVT"]}

step_info.thought:
The Well's criteria for DVT (original 2003 version) assigns points based on clinical features. Based on the documentation:
1. Active cancer: No (0 points)
2. Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilization of the lower extremities: No (0 points)
3. Recently bedridden for 3 days or more, or major surgery within 12 weeks: No (0 points)
4. Localized tenderness along the distribution of the deep venous system: No (0 points)
5. Entire leg swollen: Yes (1 point)
6. Calf swelling at least 3 cm larger than the asymptomatic side: Yes (1 point)
7. Pitting edema confined to the symptomatic leg: No (0 points)
8. Collateral superficial veins (non-varicose): No (0 points)
9. Previously documented DVT: Yes (1 point)
10. Alternative diagnosis at least as likely as DVT: No, the exam and findings indicate no other obvious cause (0 points).

Summing these points: 1 (entire leg swollen) + 1 (calf swelling >3cm) + 1 (prior DVT) = 3 points. A score of 3 indicates a "High Probability" for DVT.

args: A 61-year-old right-hand-dominant man with a medical history notable for non-insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic atrial fibrillation on apixaban, stage 2 chronic kidney disease, and remote left hip arthroplasty was transported to the emergency department by county fire EMS after falling approximately 8 feet from an aluminum extension ladder while trimming tree branches in his driveway at 08:10 this morning. A neighbor who heard the crash found him supine on concrete with pruning shears beside him; he was breathing, had a palpable carotid pulse, but his eyes were closed and he produced only low-pitched moans that did not form recognizable words. No seizure-like shaking, tongue biting, or emesis was witnessed and there was no report of headstrike beyond the presumed impact to the occiput when he landed. Paramedics arrived at 08:18, applied a rigid cervical collar, secured him supine to a vacuum mattress with head blocks, placed a 16-gauge IV in the right antecubital fossa but ran only a 10 mL saline lock, and administered no fentanyl, midazolam, ketamine, paralytics, dextrose, naloxone, or vasopressors so that the initial neurologic examination would remain unaltered. A finger-stick glucose was 118 mg/dL, and there was no obvious alcohol odor. During the twelve-minute transport he received 12 L/min oxygen through a non-rebreather mask and remained hemodynamically stable. Upon arrival to our resuscitation bay at 08:34, immediately prior to any hospital therapy, vital signs are blood pressure 146/82 mm Hg obtained in the left brachial artery, heart rate 94 beats per minute in an irregularly irregular rhythm on the monitor, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute with shallow but regular chest rise, oxygen saturation 99 % on the pre-hospital mask, and rectal temperature 36.6 °C. The patient lies motionless on the EMS backboard, work boots still laced, grey sweatshirt damp with morning dew, and a 4-cm linear scalp laceration with scant oozing is noted at the right occipital rim. Initial airway survey shows the mouth partially open with intact dentition, midsize tongue, and dried blood coating the posterior palate; a Yankauer catheter gently suctioning the oropharynx triggers a brisk gag followed by a muffled groan. Throughout quiet observation his eyelids remain firmly shut. When the trauma nurse calls his first and last name at conversational volume there is no change, but after the name is repeated loudly near his right ear both lids part to mid-palpebral distance, revealing pupils 3 mm bilaterally that constrict briskly to direct and consensual light; once questioning ceases, the lids drift closed again. He does not follow commands such as “show two fingers” or “wiggle your toes.” Speech is absent; instead, every 20–30 seconds a deep, throaty moan escapes, devoid of articulation, syllable formation, or recognizable language. Cervical collar remains in place and neck is non-mobile for the exam. Upper extremities lie slack along his torso, but when firm nail-bed pressure is applied to the left index finger both shoulders adduct, elbows sweep rapidly into pronounced flexion, forearms pronate, and wrists flex with fists partially clenched; lower limbs simultaneously extend at the knees with plantar flexion of the ankles. The posture endures two to three seconds beyond cessation of the stimulus before limbs relax back to baseline. A second noxious stimulus—a sternal rub—elicits an identical symmetric response without any purposeful attempt to reach toward or push away the examiner’s hand. Tone between stimuli is otherwise reduced, and no spontaneous movement of the trunk or extremities is observed. Deep tendon reflexes are 3 + at biceps and patellae, 2 + at Achilles; plantar responses are mute. Corneal reflexes are intact, extra-ocular movements appear conjugate when the globes are gently rotated, and a grimace is noted when supra-orbital ridge pressure is applied. Cardiovascular examination reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and distal pulses 2 + with capillary refill <2 seconds. Lungs are clear to auscultation with equal breath sounds and resonant percussion. Abdomen is flat, soft, and nontender; bowel sounds are present. Pelvis is stable to gentle compression, and rectal tone is preserved. No obvious long-bone deformities are present, though mild ecchymosis is forming over the right olecranon and left lateral thigh. A focused log-roll demonstrates no vertebral step-off or paraspinal hematoma, and posterior chest wall inspection shows scattered superficial abrasions. Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph obtained within five minutes reveals clear lung fields and normal mediastinum; pelvic radiograph is negative for fracture. Point-of-care venous blood gas on the current oxygen flow shows pH 7.36, pCO₂ 41 mm Hg, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, and lactate 2.1 mmol/L. Baseline laboratories—including complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, coagulation profile, serum ethanol level, high-sensitivity troponin, and type & screen—have been drawn but are pending. No analgesics, sedatives, paralytics, hyperosmolar agents, vasopressors, or intravenous fluids have yet been administered in the department, ensuring that the neurologic findings described—eyes that only open after loud verbal stimulus, persistent unintelligible groaning in lieu of speech, and a characteristic bilateral flexor limb reaction to painful stimulation—represent his untreated baseline at presentation. Neurosurgery, trauma surgery, and critical care have been emergently paged; non-contrast head CT, CT angiography of the neck, and whole-spine CT are ordered stat, and serial neurologic checks every fifteen minutes have been instituted while airway equipment is prepared at bedside should his protective reflexes diminish.

step_info.thought:
To determine the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score, we need to evaluate the patient's Eye (E), Verbal (V), and Motor (M) responses based on the provided clinical documentation at the time of arrival.

1.  **Eye Response (E):** The note states the patient's eyes are shut during quiet observation and do not respond to conversational volume, but the lids part after being spoken to loudly. According to GCS criteria, opening eyes to sound/speech corresponds to a score of **3**.
2.  **Verbal Response (V):** The patient produces only unintelligible deep, throaty moans devoid of articulation or recognizable language. This corresponds to a GCS verbal score of **2**.
3.  **Motor Response (M):** Upon noxious stimulation (nail-bed pressure/sternal rub), the patient exhibits abnormal flexion (decorticate posturing: elbows flexed, wrists/fists clenched, shoulders adducted). This corresponds to a GCS motor score of **3**.

The total GCS score is E3 + V2 + M3 = 8.

args:
A 22-month-old African-American girl was transferred from an outside hospital to the intensive care unit for further evaluation and the management of uncontrolled severe hypertension. She had a three-day history of fever, cough, rhinorrhea, shortness of breath, dizziness and vomiting. She was born at term with a weight of 2.8 kg and had an unremarkable perinatal course. She had no known genetic syndromes. Besides a history of difficulty gaining weight with a current weight of 10.7 kg (seventh percentile) and a height of the 25th centile, she had no other known medical problems in the past. There was no history of urinary tract infections or blood in urine. She did not have headaches, seizures or fainting episodes. Immunizations were up-to-date. Prior blood pressure (BP) measurements were unknown because they were never obtained. There were no other past hospitalizations. She had not been taking any medications. Family history was unremarkable for kidney disease, hypertension, dialysis or transplantation.
Upon physical examination, her vital signs were as follows: a temperature of 36.9 °C (98 °F), a respiratory rate of 16 per minute, a heart rate of 145 beats per minute, an oxygen saturation of 100% on room air, and an elevated BP of 200/120 mm Hg (manual, right upper extremity, proper size cuff; 95th percentile value for her age, gender and height 104/62 mm Hg). The examination showed a thin-appearing toddler who was not in obvious distress. The rest of the examination was negative except for tachycardia and a grade II/VI systolic murmur at the apex. A renal bruit was not heard upon auscultation. There was no skin rash or edema. The neurologic examination was normal.
The nasal swab for a respiratory viral panel test was positive for parainfluenza 3. Besides mild nasal congestion, she had no cough, difficulty breathing, or wheezing, and she remained stable in room air. She remained afebrile throughout the hospital admission. Her throat culture was negative for beta-hemolytic streptococcus. Her blood culture showed no growth. Her chest x-ray showed mild cardiomegaly but no focal airspace disease, pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Her electrocardiogram showed supraventricular tachycardia. Her echocardiogram revealed a dilated left ventricle with severe concentric left ventricular (LV) hypertrophy, with an LV mass index of 187 gm/m2.7 and a mildly depressed LV systolic function with an ejection fraction of 50%. There was a thrombus in the apex of the LV. The serum brain natriuretic peptide and troponin I levels were 1565 pg/mL (normal: <100 pg/mL) and 242 pg/mL (normal: <15 pg/mL), respectively. For possible viral-induced cardiomyopathy, she was started on intravenous immunoglobulin and methylprednisolone. For hypertension, she was started on nicardipine infusion, along with nitroprusside with a goal BP reduction of 30% over 24 hours. Milrinone was added for depressed LV systolic function. She remained on the maximum nicardipine infusion rate for several days due to persistent hypertension, with systolic BPs of 140–150 mm Hg. Heparin was transitioned to enoxaparin and aspirin, with the resolution of the cardiac thrombus by day three. A non-contrast computed tomography (CT) scan of head showed no mass, acute intracranial hemorrhage, cytotoxic edema, midline shift, or signs of herniation. Her urine output was normal.
A renal function test showed an initial serum sodium level of 131 meq/L with a nadir of 121 meq/L at day three of admission, a serum chloride level of 97 meq/L (nadir 84 meq/L at day three), a serum potassium level of 3.2 meq/L, a blood urea nitrogen level of 25 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine level of 0.5 mg/dL. Her fasting blood glucose level was 90 mg/dL. Her urine sodium level was 89 mmol/L with a fractional excretion of sodium of 1.5%. Her serum albumin level was between 2.1 and 2.5 gm/dL, and her random urine protein to creatinine ratio was 4.6 (normal: <0.2). There was no microscopic hematuria. Her urine culture showed no growth. Her blood counts and serum complements were both normal. Her sickle cell screen was negative. A renal sonogram showed a small right kidney of 6 cm in length (36th centile) and a left kidney of 7.7 cm in length (96th centile), with good blood flow in both kidneys. Her serum aldosterone and plasma renin activity at the time of admission were both elevated at 106 ng/dL (normal: <16 ng/dL, supine, quantitative chemiluminescent immunoassay) and >150 ng/mL/h, respectively (normal: 1.7–11.2 ng/mL/h, supine, quantitative enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay; both ARUP laboratories, Salt Lake City, UT, USA).
A work-up for possible catecholamine-secreting tumors due to severe hypertension included the evaluation of urine and serum catecholamines. Her 24-hour urine normetanephrine, metanephrine, vanillylmandelic acid, and homovanillic acid levels were 1525 µg/g of creatinine (normal: 0–1300 µg/g of creatinine), 433 µg/g of creatinine (normal: 0–530 µg/g of creatinine), 10 mg/g of creatinine (normal: <16 mg/g of creatinine), and 12 mg/g of creatinine (normal: <25 mg/g of creatinine), respectively. Her serum fractionated catecholamines showed the following: an epinephrine level of 152 pg/mL (normal: 36–640 pg/mL) and a norepinephrine level of 1964 pg/mL (normal: 68–1810 pg/ml), respectively. Given a less than two-fold increase from normal range of serum norepinephrine, the suspicion of the presence of a neuroendocrine tumor was low.
A diuretic nuclear renogram showed asymmetrical renal function with decreased blood flow and function in the small right kidney relative to the left. Her differential function was 70% in the left and 30% in the right. There was a normal uptake of the technetium-99 m MAG3 (mercaptoacetyltriglycine) with prompt excretion from the left kidney. Her right kidney had little delayed uptake with prompt excretion ( and , ). A duplex sonogram of her RA showed focal elevation in velocity in the proximal right main RA (peak systolic velocity (PSV): 405 cm/second) with a decreased velocity in the mid and distal RAs (PSV: 117 and 110 cm/sec, respectively). The renal aortic ratio (RAR, the ratio of renal to aortic PSV) was elevated at 3.2. Additionally, distinct “tardus-parvus” (tardus-slow and late, parvus-small and little) waveforms were seen at the right hilar and interlobar arteries. This combination of an elevated PSV at the proximal right main RA along with an elevated RAR (usual cut-off 3) and abnormal waveforms was highly suggestive of proximal right main RAS. Velocities in the left main RA were normal with no differences at various segments of the main artery. A CT angiogram (CTA) of the RAs showed a small right kidney of 6 cm in length with a decreased enhancement of the superior pole of the right kidney. The origin of the right main RA was diminutive compared to the left. There was a homogenous enhancement of the left kidney with a normal appearing left RA. Hypertension at that time was well controlled with amlodipine, clonidine, furosemide, spironolactone and labetalol. Nicardipine infusion was discontinued at day seven of admission.
A few days later, she underwent a digital subtraction angiogram (DSA), which showed a beaded appearance of the right proximal main RA, which caused a focal stenosis with post-stenotic dilatation ( and ). The pressure gradient across the stenotic lesion was 40 mm Hg. There was normal renal parenchymal perfusion. The left RA demonstrated a normal appearance of the main and branch arteries with normal renal parenchymal perfusion and no evidence of stenosis and irregularity. A diagnosis of right RA stenosis, most likely due to focal FMD, was made. In conjunction with vascular surgery and intervention radiology, it was collectively decided not to pursue the intervention of the stenotic lesion due to the risk of complication, specifically the injury or dissection of the right RA, especially because her BPs were relatively controlled on five antihypertensive agents. Subsequent to the angiogram, enalapril was added once bilateral RA stenosis was excluded for optimal BP control. A CTA of the head and neck vessels to look for evidence of FMD in the head and neck showed a normal vasculature of the vessels. Baby aspirin was started to prevent thromboembolic events.
Though fever, parainfluenza virus infection, and LV thrombus could have partly contributed to the elevated BPs initially, these signs and symptoms had resolved at the time of or soon after admission. Additionally, the finding of a discrete anatomic lesion in the right RA that is known to cause hypertension suggests that the right RA FMD indeed was the most probable cause of the severe, long-standing hypertension, as suggested by the severe concentric LV hypertrophy. At the time of discharge two weeks later, her BPs were relatively optimal (105–110 mm Hg SBPs) on enalapril, amlodipine and labetalol. Subsequent monthly clinic follow-up visits seven months later have shown stable SBPs with no clinical symptoms of hypertension. Her renal function has remained stable with normal serum electrolytes without hyponatremia and hypokalemia. Her proteinuria has resolved. A follow-up echocardiogram showed normal LV systolic function and stable but persistent LV hypertrophy with no reappearance of the thrombus that was seen in the first echocardiogram. For now, we plan to closely follow-her BPs, along with a follow-up diuretic renogram and a DSA in a few months, with a tentative plan for either continued medical treatment or surgical intervention (angioplasty with or without stent and surgical revascularization or right nephrectomy) based on the clinical course.

She had a three-day history of fever, cough, rhinorrhea, shortness of breath, dizziness and vomiting. ... Her throat culture was negative for beta-hemolytic streptococcus... Her heart rate of 145 beats per minute... respiratory rate of 16 per minute... temperature of 36.9 °C... grade II/VI systolic murmur... no cough...", "required_values": ["age", "fever", "cough", "tonsillar_exudate", "swollen_tender_anterior_cervical_nodes"]}

output.reasoning: The question directly asks for the Centor Score, which is the focus of the available calculator.

output.error: Calculation failed for Centor Score (McIsaac) with values {'cough': 1, 'fever': 1, 'swollen_tender_anterior_cervical_nodes': 0, 'tonsillar_exudate': 0, 'age': 1.83}

step_info.thought: The Centor (McIsaac) Score uses specific clinical criteria: fever (>38°C), lack of cough, swollen/tender anterior cervical nodes, tonsillar exudates, and an age modifier. The patient was 22 months old (1.83 years), had fever (initially), but also had a cough, no reported tonsillar exudate or cervical lymphadenopathy, and the throat culture was negative. The age modifier in the McIsaac score adds 1 point for age 3-14, 0 for age 15-44, and -1 for age >=45; for a 22-month-old, the score contribution is generally 0. Based on the criteria (Fever=+1, Cough=+1 (which negates the fever point or is simply not "no cough"), Tonsillar Exudate=0, Nodes=0, Age=0), the total score is 0.

args:
A 29-years-old female experienced an episode of mild upper respiratory tract infection followed by a slowly developing dysphagia and dyspnea. This led to impaired exercise tolerance lasting several months, with recent subacute exacerbation. There was no significant history of travel or exposure to infectious agents. She had not suffered from fever or other acute or chronic infectious symptoms. Her palatine tonsils had been removed in childhood. She was diagnosed with epilepsy at age 17 and had been free of epileptic symptoms for over 5 years with levetiracetam (500 mg two times per day) and lamotrigine (150 mg two times per day). Her family history was unremarkable.
She spoke with hoarse voice, without signs or findings that would have suggested systemic involvement. There were no signs of generalized mucosal disease. Fiberoptic examination showed swelling of the lingual tonsil, epiglottic and arytenoid mucosa, causing airway obstruction (Fig. a). C-reactive protein concentration and blood sedimentation rate were low, and anti-nuclear, anti-neutrophil, anti-glomerular basement membrane, anti-myeloperoxidase, anti-proteinase 3, tissue transglutaminase and cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies were negative. Thyroid function was normal and thyroid peroxidase antibodies were 34 IU/ml (normal value < 60 IU/ml). Plasma parathyroid hormone (39 ng/l; normal 18–80 ng/l) and serum vitamin D-25 (77 nmol/l; normal > 50 nmol/l) were normal. No evidence for acute or chronic viral, bacterial, mycobacterial or fungal infections, including hepatitis B and C, human immunodeficiency virus and tularemia, was found.
Due to her swollen lingual tonsil and laryngeal mucosa causing airway obstruction, she was hospitalized and received empiric cefuroxime (1.5 g thrice daily) and methylprednisolone (75 mg once daily, height 173 cm, weight 63 kg, prednisolone dose 1.2 mg/kg) intravenously. After a transient positive response, she was discharged from the hospital after 6 days. Soon after, she was readmitted to due to a rapid reoccurrence of the symptoms. A modest positive response was seen after per oral clindamycin and oral prednisolone (60 mg/days) with dose reduction for 14 days. Lingual tonsil and epiglottis were biopsied. Histology showed granulomatous reaction in lingual tonsil with CD68+ (Kp-1) epithelioid histiocytes (Fig. ). The CD20+ B lymphocyte count was increased while Pax-5+ positive B lymphocyte count was normal. CD138+ plasma cells were scarce, and they showed polytypic kappa- and lambda light chain expression. The number of CD3+ T cells was normal, and most T cells were CD4+. Eosinophils were practically absent and well-formed follicles were not seen. Epstein–Barr virus in situ hybridization (EBER), p16, cytomegalovirus and mycobacteria were negative. Morphology, immunohistochemical staining, serum electrophoresis and bone marrow aspirate did not raise suspicion of lymphoid neoplasia, thus, no clonality studies were done.
Due to granulomas, sarcoidosis was considered. However, chest X-ray, computerized tomography of chest and ultrasound of neck and abdomen were unremarkable. Positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) showed increased fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake in the lingual tonsil, without evidence of widespread sarcoidosis (Fig. ). No other pathological FDG foci were found. Focal physiological FDG accumulation was seen in ureters on both sides of the spinal column. This is a common finding due to ureteral peristalsis and pooling of radiotracer in the recumbent patient. The patient had no neurological symptoms and brain magnetic resonance imaging was normal. Serum angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) was within normal range (11–20 U/l; reference range 9–65 U/l) while daily urine calcium output was normal or slightly elevated (5.0–6.31 mmol; reference range 1.2–5.5 mmol).
Subsequently, an unusual presentation of primary or secondary immunodeficiency was considered. There was no consanguinity; the patient or her family had no history of repeated infections or autoimmunity. White cell, lymphocyte, and B, T and NK lymphocyte subset counts were within normal limits. CD19+ B cell count was 153 × 109/l (normal range 80–616 × 109/l). Proportions of memory CD27+ B-cells (30.3%) and IgD−IgM−CD27+ switched memory B cells 7.2%; (normal range 6.5–29.2%) were normal and the percentage of activated CD21low B cells was high (6.6%; normal range 0.6–3.5%). Percentages of various CD3+CD4+ and CD3+CD8+ T cell subsets appeared normal. Serum total IgG (3.5 g/l; normal range 6.77–15.0 g/l) and all IgG subclass concentrations were low: IgG1 2.45 g/l (normal range 4.9–11.4 g/l), IgG2 0.91 g/l (normal range 1.5–6.4 g/l), IgG3 0.18 g/l (normal range 0.2–1.1 g/l) and IgG4 0.02 g/L (normal range 0.08–1.4 g/l). Anti-polysaccharide response to Pneumovax® were impaired, with only 3/10 serotypes reaching a level of 0.35 µg/ml after immunization. However, serum IgA (0.90 g/l; normal range 0.52–4.02 g/l) and IgM concentrations (0.57 g/l; normal range 0.47–2.84 g/l) were normal. Dihydrorhodamine test excluded chronic granulomatous disease. These findings were thus consistent with either granulomatous CVID or most likely secondary drug-induced hypogammaglobulinemia with a novel clinical presentation [–]. Genetic analysis (Blueprint Genetics, Primary Immunodeficiency Plus panel. ) did not find known mutations causative of CVID-like or other primary immunodeficiencies.
Due to symptomatic upper airway obstruction, dysphagia, lack of evident infection and unsatisfactory response to prednisolone treatment and possible granulomatous CVID, the patient received immunoglobulin replacement and rituximab therapy (Mabthera® 100 mg, 200 mg and 500 mg on three consecutive days combined with 100 mg hydrocortisone and 1 g paracetamol), with a favorable response within 2 weeks accompanied by significant improvement in dysphagia and respiratory symptoms. Seven months later, her exercise capacity was good. She has remained asymptomatic for over 16 months after rituximab treatment. Her fiberoptic pharyngeal findings improved (Fig. ). She continues to receive subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement with serum IgG levels in the range of 10 to 12 g/l.
What is the patient's FeverPAIN score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

{"patient_note": "A 29-years-old female experienced an episode of mild upper respiratory tract infection followed by a slowly developing dysphagia and dyspnea. This led to impaired exercise tolerance lasting several months, with recent subacute exacerbation. There was no significant history of travel or exposure to infectious agents. She had not suffered from fever or other acute or chronic infectious symptoms. Her palatine tonsils had been removed in childhood. She was diagnosed with epilepsy at age 17 and had been free of epileptic symptoms for over 5 years with levetiracetam (500 mg two times per day) and lamotrigine (150 mg two times per day). Her family history was unremarkable.
She spoke with hoarse voice, without signs or findings that would have suggested systemic involvement. There were no signs of generalized mucosal disease. Fiberoptic examination showed swelling of the lingual tonsil, epiglottic and arytenoid mucosa, causing airway obstruction (Fig. a). C-reactive protein concentration and blood sedimentation rate were low, and anti-nuclear, anti-neutrophil, anti-glomerular basement membrane, anti-myeloperoxidase, anti-proteinase 3, tissue transglutaminase and cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies were negative. Thyroid function was normal and thyroid peroxidase antibodies were 34 IU/ml (normal value < 60 IU/ml). Plasma parathyroid hormone (39 ng/l; normal 18–80 ng/l) and serum vitamin D-25 (77 nmol/l; normal > 50 nmol/l) were normal. No evidence for acute or chronic viral, bacterial, mycobacterial or fungal infections, including hepatitis B and C, human immunodeficiency virus and tularemia, was found.
Due to her swollen lingual tonsil and laryngeal mucosa causing airway obstruction, she was hospitalized and received empiric cefuroxime (1.5 g thrice daily) and methylprednisolone (75 mg once daily, height 173 cm, weight 63 kg, prednisolone dose 1.2 mg/kg) intravenously. After a transient positive response, she was discharged from the hospital after 6 days. Soon after, she was readmitted to due to a rapid reoccurrence of the symptoms. A modest positive response was seen after per oral clindamycin and oral prednisolone (60 mg/days) with dose reduction for 14 days. Lingual tonsil and epiglottis were biopsied. Histology showed granulomatous reaction in lingual tonsil with CD68+ (Kp-1) epithelioid histiocytes (Fig. ). The CD20+ B lymphocyte count was increased while Pax-5+ positive B lymphocyte count was normal. CD138+ plasma cells were scarce, and they showed polytypic kappa- and lambda light chain expression. The note explicitly states she had "not suffered from fever" (Fever = 0) and the fiberoptic examination showed "swelling of the lingual tonsil" (Inflamed tonsils = 1). Other criteria (purulence, attending rapidly, and no cough) are not mentioned or cannot be inferred as present, and are thus assigned 0. Therefore, the FeverPAIN score is 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 1.

calculator_name: HAS-BLED Score for Major Bleeding Risk

args: A 48-year-old man arrived to the cardiac rhythm clinic for an initial counseling visit regarding long-term oral anticoagulation that had been prescribed three months earlier when electrocardiographic monitoring for intermittent palpitations unexpectedly demonstrated non-valvular atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response. He describes himself as a full-time software engineer who bicycles to work when the weather permits and generally feels healthy, but he has been told for several years that his blood pressure trends high; his primary care clinician started lisinopril 20 mg daily two years ago, yet home cuff readings remain in the mid-140s/90s and in clinic today a seated manual measurement after five minutes of rest is 156/94 mm Hg with a repeat value of 152/92 mm Hg, heart rate irregularly irregular at 86 beats per minute, respirations 16 per minute, temperature 36.5 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. He states that at age 46 he was hospitalized overnight after passing a large, tarry stool and becoming light-headed while playing basketball; hemoglobin at that time was 6.9 g/dL, upper endoscopy revealed an actively spurting posterior duodenal ulcer that required epinephrine injection and clip placement, and he received two units of packed red blood cells before discharge on a twice-daily proton-pump inhibitor regimen. Since that episode he has experienced no further melena, hematemesis, epistaxis, hematuria, or easy bruising and his baseline hemoglobin has remained in the 14 g/dL range, but the memory of that bleeding event makes him anxious about taking “blood thinners.” He denies any prior ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke, transient ischemic attack, carotid disease, or peripheral embolic phenomena and has no family history of cerebral aneurysm or bleeding diathesis. Other medical problems include borderline dyslipidemia managed with dietary modification alone and mild seasonal allergic rhinitis treated with an as-needed intranasal corticosteroid; he has never been diagnosed with diabetes, chronic kidney disease, viral hepatitis, fatty liver, cirrhosis, or pancreatitis. Review of systems is negative for jaundice, pruritus, right-upper-quadrant pain, confusion, abdominal distension, ankle swelling, or exercise limitation. He reports consuming alcohol socially—two craft beers most weeknights after work and an additional three or four drinks during weekend gatherings—totaling about 13 alcoholic drinks per week; he denies binge patterns, blackouts, withdrawal symptoms, or any counseling for alcohol reduction. He quit smoking ten years ago after a five-pack-year history and has never used recreational drugs. Current medications include apixaban 5 mg twice daily, lisinopril as noted, cetirizine 10 mg each evening during pollen season, and omeprazole 40 mg every morning; he specifically avoids over-the-counter nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents because of his prior ulcer and does not take aspirin, clopidogrel, dipyridamole, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, corticosteroids, or herbal supplements geared toward “natural blood thinning.” He brings a log from his community laboratory showing apixaban anti-Xa levels checked opportunistically for research enrollment have been within the expected reference range on three occasions, and routine coagulation studies obtained by his primary physician one week ago noted an international normalized ratio of 1.1, activated partial thromboplastin time 28 seconds, platelet count 245 × 10⁹/L, aspartate aminotransferase 22 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 24 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 70 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, albumin 4.3 g/dL, and hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL. Physical examination today reveals a lean man in no distress, body mass index 24 kg/m², with anicteric sclerae, moist mucous membranes without petechiae, a supple neck absent jugular venous distention, clear lung fields, a nondisplaced point of maximal impulse, variable S1 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and a soft nontender abdomen without hepatosplenomegaly or ascites; there is no peripheral edema, cyanosis, ecchymosis, or palpable purpura, and neurologic testing shows intact cranial nerves, normal coordination, and steady gait. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained during the visit confirms atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular rate of 82 bpm, normal axis, and no acute ischemic changes, while an echocardiogram performed last month documented normal biventricular size and function with mild left atrial enlargement and no significant valvular disease. After a detailed discussion, he expresses desire to better control his blood pressure, continue avoidance of agents that could enhance bleeding risk, moderate his alcohol consumption to below current levels, and obtain clarification from gastroenterology regarding long-term ulcer prophylaxis while he remains on anticoagulation.
What is the patient's HAS-BLED score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args: {"available_calculators": ["HAS-BLED Score"], "question": "What is the patient's HAS-BLED score?"}

output.reasoning: HAS-BLED score explicitly mentioned in the question

step_info.thought:
The HAS-BLED score evaluates major bleeding risk in patients with atrial fibrillation using clinical criteria. Based on the patient note, I have identified the following:
- Hypertension (H): Patient has a history of high blood pressure and is on lisinopril, so this criteria is met (+1).
- Abnormal renal/liver function (A): Patient has no history of liver or kidney disease and labs are normal, so this is not met (0).
- Stroke (S): No history of stroke, so this is not met (0).
- Bleeding history (B): Patient had a significant upper gastrointestinal bleed (posterior duodenal ulcer), so this criteria is met (+1).
- Labile INR (L): Not applicable for apixaban (0).
- Elderly (E): Patient is 48 years old, so not met (0).
- Drugs/Alcohol (D): Patient is currently on an anticoagulant (apixaban) which increases bleeding risk, so this criteria is met (+1); he does not meet the criteria for alcohol excess (consuming 13 drinks/week is generally below the clinical threshold for "excess" in this context).

The sum of these points (H+B+D) is 3.

args: A 24-year-old man with no significant past medical history presents to urgent care with a cough and fatigue. The patient states his symptoms have persisted for the past 48 hours and have made work difficult for him. He has been taking an antibiotic he had left over from a previous infection but states it did not improve his symptoms. His temperature is 101°F (38.3°C), blood pressure is 127/68 mmHg, pulse is 110/min, respirations are 17/min, and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. A chest radiograph is perfomed as seen in Figure A. Physical exam is notable for obesity, gynecomastia, and the finding in Figure B as well as some erythema of the posterior oropharynx.

The Centor (McIsaac) score criteria are:
1. Fever > 38°C (+1)
2. Absence of cough (+1)
3. Tonsillar exudates (+1)
4. Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy (+1)
5. Age (3-14: +1, 15-44: 0, >=45: -1)

Based on the note:
- Fever: Yes (38.3°C, +1)
- Cough: The note explicitly states the patient presents with a cough (+0)
- Tonsillar exudate: Not mentioned (+0)
- Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy: Not mentioned (+0)
- Age: 24 (+0)

Total score = 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 1.

args:
A 44-year-old female patient admitted to the university hospital for new-onset left-sided weakness and right-sided headache; dizziness, confusion, chest pain, and palpitations for four days; and generalized malaise for two months. Medical history was significant for hypertension, chronic hepatitis C treatment naive, hypothyroidism, recent IV drug abuse, and alcoholism, and she was a former heavy smoker. A CT scan (computerized tomography) of the head done at the outside hospital revealed acute infarctions in the right thalamus and right temporal lobe. EKG showed normal sinus rhythm, and a chest X-ray was normal.
On the day of admission, vital signs were blood pressure of 118/74 mmHg, pulse rate of 117/minute, respiratory rate of 20/minute, temperature of 38°C, and SpO2 of 98% on room air. Physical examination revealed the presence of poor oral hygiene and grade 3/6 systolic murmur at the cardiac apex. Neurological examination affirmed slurred speech, left-sided weakness with motor strength of 1/5 in upper and lower extremities, increased reflexes on the left side compared to the right, and right pupil dilation with sluggish response to light and right lateral ophthalmoparesis. EKG showed sinus tachycardia. TTE (transthoracic echocardiogram) () displayed an ejection fraction of 65%, dilated left atria, and severe mitral valve regurgitation with a 2 cm strand-like hypoechoic structure on its atrial surface suggestive of vegetation. CT angiogram of the head and neck displayed the patent carotid and vertebral basilar arterial system. Multifocal acute infarcts were detected at the right medial temporal lobe, right thalamus, right lateral pons, and midbrain with no hemorrhagic transformation on MRI (magnetic resonance imaging). TEE (transesophageal echocardiogram) () on day 4 revealed no evidence of thrombus or mass, ruptured chordae tendineae of the posterior mitral leaflet, and a small mobile density on the anterior mitral leaflet with no patent foramen ovale. Blood cultures obtained on day 3 of hospitalization for fever of 38.7°C resulted positive on day 5 for Gram-positive cocci in chains in 4 out of 4 bottles.
The infectious disease team was consulted on day 4 of admission. The intravenous (IV) antibiotic vancomycin 1.5 g every 12 hrs was initiated on day 5 to achieve a target vancomycin trough of 15 to 20 mcg/ml. Overall, blood cultures obtained on days 3 and 5 resulted positive in 4 out of 4 bottles. MALDI-TOF MS (matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization-time of flight mass spectrometry) was used to identify the organism due to difficulty in retrieving it from culture media. G. adiacens was confirmed on day 6 in all of the positive blood culture samples obtained. Isolated colonies determined insufficient to be transferred to a tertiary lab for antimicrobial susceptibility. IV vancomycin was continued as per treatment plan. From day 6 onwards, blood cultures remained negative for any growth. A cardiothoracic surgeon evaluated the patient for mitral valve replacement/repair surgery.
On day 9, the patient complained of acute low back pain. A CT scan of the lumbar spine with contrast revealed L3-L4 early discitis and osteomyelitis. On the same day, the patient's confusion worsened and was transferred to the neuroscience intensive care unit. The MRI brain was repeated for deteriorating confusion. It () revealed right thalamic and right medial temporal-occipital abscess (measuring 1.1 × 2.3 × 2.9 cm) associated with extensive vasogenic edema extending to the brainstem. Lumbar spine MRI () displayed L3-L4 discitis with osteomyelitis with no epidural or paravertebral abscess. The CSF (cerebrospinal fluid) analysis revealed an elevated total protein of 196 mg/dl, decreased glucose of 33 mg/dl, elevated white cell count of 385/mcl, lymphocyte predominant (85%), and RBC of 1040/mcl. The CSF BioFire panel for meningitis pathogens was negative. At this time, IV meropenem 2 g every 8 hrs was started alongside with vancomycin. The patient's confusion gradually cleared with improvement in speech and left-side strength. Cardiac catheterization revealed normal coronary arteries without any significant disease. Normal coronary arteries implied that ruptured chordae tendineae was due to infection. Cardiothoracic surgery reevaluated the patient and recommended transfer to a larger tertiary care facility due to the complex clinical condition and high surgical risk. Vancomycin trough (mcg/ml) was 14.6 on day 7, 21.4 on day 14, and 24.8 on day 20.
The tertiary care center transfer was on day 21 in a clinically stable condition. IV vancomycin and meropenem continued at transfer. Mitral valve replacement with Hancock type 2 MV tissue was done on day 31, and the patient was discharged on six-week course of IV vancomycin and ertapenem. On clinical follow-up at three months, mild residual weakness on the left side was noted, and brain imaging revealed resolving abscesses.
What are the number of SIRS critiera met by the patient? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

A 46-year-old man with morbid obesity, insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes, chronic hypertension, and longstanding obstructive sleep apnea normally treated with a home auto-titrating device was brought in from his workplace after suddenly developing severe shortness of breath while climbing one flight of stairs; coworkers noted he became diaphoretic, cyanotic about the lips, and unable to speak full sentences. Emergency medical technicians arrived within ten minutes to find him sitting forward in a chair, tachypneic at 30 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 83 % on room air, and speaking only in two- or three-word phrases but following commands. They applied a tight-fitting oronasal circuit that provided continuous distending pressure of 10 cm H₂O through an air-oxygen blender set to 0.43 inspired fraction; after five minutes the saturation rose to 92 %, and he remained on that interface throughout transport. No sedatives, diuretics, nitrates, bronchodilators, steroids, pressors, or intravenous fluids were administered in the field. On arrival to the resuscitation bay, before any hospital therapy was begun, he was seated upright, still wearing the same positive-pressure mask, drawing his own breaths, and able to answer questions but intermittently pausing for air. A right radial arterial sample obtained within three minutes revealed pH 7.34, PaCO₂ 41 mm Hg, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, and a PaO₂ of 84 mm Hg while the delivered oxygen concentration remained 43 %. Simultaneous vital signs documented temperature 37.0 °C tympanic, heart rate 106 beats per minute in regular sinus rhythm, blood pressure 114/72 mm Hg confirmed manually, respiratory rate 28 breaths per minute with moderate accessory-muscle use, and pulse oximetry fluctuating between 91 % and 93 % on the mask. He opened eyes spontaneously, answered orientation questions accurately but complained that “everything feels fuzzy,” and obeyed commands without hesitation, giving an overall Glasgow Coma Score of 14. Pupils were 3 mm bilaterally and briskly reactive, extraocular movements were full, and no focal motor deficits were discerned on quick pronator-drift testing. Skin was warm, mottling absent, and capillary refill under two seconds. Cardiac examination demonstrated a regular rhythm with a prominent S3 gallop but no murmurs; jugular venous distension to the angle of the mandible at 45° suggested volume overload. Breath sounds revealed diffuse fine crackles up to mid-scapula bilaterally without wheeze, and percussion was dull at the bases. The abdomen was protuberant yet soft, nontender, and without hepatosplenomegaly. Lower extremities displayed bilateral pitting edema to the mid-shin but no calf tenderness. A 16-gauge peripheral intravenous catheter and right radial arterial line were placed, though no medications or fluids had yet been infused when laboratory draws were sent. Initial complete blood count showed white-cell count 11.8 × 10³/µL, hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, and a platelet count of 118 000 /µL. Serum chemistry returned sodium 136 mmol/L, potassium 4.7 mmol/L, chloride 98 mmol/L, anion gap 14, blood urea nitrogen 36 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.75 mg/dL compared with a baseline of 1.2 mg/dL from six months earlier. Total bilirubin was 1.25 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 38 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 35 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 104 U/L, and international normalized ratio 1.1. Venous lactate measured 2.2 mmol/L. A bedside cardiac ultrasound performed before any pharmacologic intervention revealed an ejection fraction around 35 %, concentric left-ventricular hypertrophy, and a plethoric inferior vena cava with less than 10 % respiratory variation. Lung ultrasound demonstrated multiple B-lines in every scanned zone, supporting pulmonary congestion. Portable chest radiograph obtained moments later showed bilateral symmetrical perihilar hazy opacities consistent with edema, mild cardiomegaly, and no focal consolidation or pleural air. A 14-French Foley catheter inserted during the secondary survey immediately evacuated 90 mL of clear yellow urine; review of the patient’s digital glucose-monitoring log, which he uses to track fluids, along with his wife’s report indicated approximately 1 150 mL of urine over the preceding 24 hours. Electrocardiogram displayed sinus tachycardia at 108 beats per minute with left-ventricular strain pattern but no acute ischemic changes. At the moment all the above physiologic and biochemical data were captured, the patient remained spontaneously breathing on the same mask delivering 10 cm H₂O continuous pressure and 43 % oxygen, maintained a blood pressure of 114/72 mm Hg without vasoactive support, demonstrated mild thrombocytopenia, modest renal impairment with creatinine 1.75 mg/dL, near-normal total bilirubin of 1.25 mg/dL, a PaO₂ of 84 mm Hg under those conditions, and had produced just over a liter of urine in the past day.
What is the patient's Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) Score?

step_info.args: {"required_values": ["pa_o2_mm_hg", "fio2_fraction", "gcs_score", "map_mm_hg", "platelets_count", "bilirubin_mg_dl", "creatinine_mg_dl", "urine_output_ml_day", "lactate_mmol_l"], "patient_note": "A 46-year-old man with morbid obesity, insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes, chronic hypertension, and longstanding obstructive sleep apnea normally treated with a home auto-titrating device was brought in from his workplace after suddenly developing severe shortness of breath while climbing one flight of stairs; coworkers noted he became diaphoretic, cyanotic about the lips, and unable to speak full sentences. Emergency medical technicians arrived within ten minutes to find him sitting forward in a chair, tachypneic at 30 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 83 % on room air, and speaking only in two- or three-word phrases but following commands. They applied a tight-fitting oronasal circuit that provided continuous distending pressure of 10 cm H₂O through an air-oxygen blender set to 0.43 inspired fraction; after five minutes the saturation rose to 92 %, and he remained on that interface throughout transport. No sedatives, diuretics, nitrates, bronchodilators, steroids, pressors, or intravenous fluids were administered in the field. On arrival to the resuscitation bay, before any hospital therapy was begun, he was seated upright, still wearing the same positive-pressure mask, drawing his own breaths, and able to answer questions but intermittently pausing for air. A right radial arterial sample obtained within three minutes revealed pH 7.34, PaCO₂ 41 mm Hg, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, and a PaO₂ of 84 mm Hg while the delivered oxygen concentration remained 43 %. Simultaneous vital signs documented temperature 37.0 °C tympanic, heart rate 106 beats per minute in regular sinus rhythm, blood pressure 114/72 mm Hg confirmed manually, respiratory rate 28 breaths per minute with moderate accessory-muscle use, and pulse oximetry fluctuating between 91 % and 93 % on the mask. He opened eyes spontaneously, answered orientation questions accurately but complained that “everything feels fuzzy,” and obeyed commands without hesitation, giving an overall Glasgow Coma Score of 14. Pupils were 3 mm bilaterally and briskly reactive, extraocular movements were full, and no focal motor deficits were discerned on quick pronator-drift testing. Skin was warm, mottling absent, and capillary refill under two seconds. Cardiac examination demonstrated a regular rhythm with a prominent S3 gallop but no murmurs; jugular venous distension to the angle of the mandible at 45° suggested volume overload. Breath sounds revealed diffuse fine crackles up to mid-scapula bilaterally without wheeze, and percussion was dull at the bases. The abdomen was protuberant yet soft, nontender, and without hepatosplenomegaly. Lower extremities displayed bilateral pitting edema to the mid-shin but no calf tenderness. A 16-gauge peripheral intravenous catheter and right radial arterial line were placed, though no medications or fluids had yet been infused when laboratory draws were sent. Initial complete blood count showed white-cell count 11.8 × 10³/µL, hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, and a platelet count of 118 000 /µL. Serum chemistry returned sodium 136 mmol/L, potassium 4.7 mmol/L, chloride 98 mmHg, anion gap 14, blood urea nitrogen 36 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.75 mg/dL compared with a baseline of 1.2 mg/dL from six months earlier. Total bilirubin was 1.25 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 38 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 35 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 104 U/L, and international normalized ratio 1.1. Venous lactate measured 2.2 mmol/L. A bedside cardiac ultrasound performed before any pharmacologic intervention revealed an ejection fraction around 35 %, concentric left-ventricular hypertrophy, and a plethoric inferior vena cava with less than 10 % respiratory variation. Lung ultrasound demonstrated multiple B-lines in every scanned zone, supporting pulmonary congestion. Portable chest radiograph obtained moments later showed bilateral symmetrical perihilar hazy opacities consistent with edema, mild cardiomegaly, and no focal consolidation or pleural air. A 14-French Foley catheter inserted during the secondary survey immediately evacuated 90 mL of clear yellow urine; review of the patient’s digital glucose-monitoring log, which he uses to track fluids, along with his wife’s report indicated approximately 1 150 mL of urine over the preceding 24 hours. Electrocardiogram displayed sinus tachycardia at 108 beats per minute with left-ventricular strain pattern but no acute ischemic changes. At the moment all the above physiologic and biochemical data were captured, the patient remained spontaneously breathing on the same mask delivering 10 cm H₂O continuous pressure and 43 % oxygen, maintained a blood pressure of 114/72 mm Hg without vasoactive support, demonstrated mild thrombocytopenia, modest renal impairment with creatinine 1.75 mg/dL, near-normal total bilirubin of 1.25 mg/dL, a PaO₂ of 84 mm Hg under those conditions, and had produced just over a liter of urine in the past day.', required_values:"}

step_info.thought:
The SOFA score is calculated based on six organ systems: respiratory (PaO2/FiO2), nervous (GCS), cardiovascular (MAP or vasopressors), coagulation (platelets), liver (bilirubin), and renal (creatinine or urine output).

1.  **Respiratory:** PaO2 = 84 mmHg, FiO2 = 0.43. PaO2/FiO2 = 84 / 0.43 ≈ 195.35. A ratio < 200 corresponds to 3 points.
2.  **Nervous:** GCS = 14. GCS 13-14 corresponds to 1 point.
3.  **Cardiovascular:** MAP = (2 * 72 + 114) / 3 = 86.67 mmHg. MAP >= 70 mmHg corresponds to 0 points.
4.  **Coagulation:** Platelets = 118,000 /µL. 100,000 <= Platelets < 150,000 corresponds to 1 point.
5.  **Liver:** Bilirubin = 1.25 mg/dL. 1.2 <= Bilirubin < 2.0 corresponds to 1 point.
6.  **Renal:** Creatinine = 1.75 mg/dL. 1.2 <= Creatinine < 2.0 corresponds to 1 point.

Summing these: 3 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7.

Final SOFA score: 7.

args:
A 65-year-old male marathon runner presented to a primary care physician with abrupt onset of posterior left knee pain that began two days before. There had been no injury associated with the pain. He denied systemic symptoms, including fever, chills, night sweats, or malaise. Past medical history included asymptomatic severe mitral valve regurgitation secondary to bileaflet mitral valve prolapse, for which he underwent robot-assisted minimally invasive mitral valve repair and prosthetic ring placement approximately 6 months prior to presentation. There was also a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He lived in the central United States with his wife and was employed as an attorney. He consumed approximately two alcoholic drinks per night, was a never smoker, and had no history of intravenous drug use. There were no other significant exposures or travel documented.
The patient recovered from his mitral valve repair, had stopped anticoagulation after 6 weeks, and had returned to walking and/or running 3–5 miles per day. A transthoracic echocardiogram that was done approximately 2 months postoperatively demonstrated a normal functioning valve, with only trace regurgitation.
He was initially treated with symptomatic pain relief with hydrocodone-acetaminophen but had no improvement in the left popliteal fossa pain and was therefore referred to an orthopedic provider. A plain radiograph demonstrated Baker's cyst and he was treated with a cortisone injection. Due to persistence of symptoms, he was subsequently evaluated for possible deep venous thrombosis via venous ultrasound which indicated a left popliteal thrombus and aneurysm. As a result of this finding, he was admitted to an outside hospital for further evaluation.
At the outside facility, he underwent MRI of the left knee, CT angiogram with bilateral extremity runoff, and transthoracic echocardiogram. These studies revealed left popliteal aneurysm with occlusive eccentric thrombus and incomplete clot of a right renal artery branch. The following day, a transthoracic echocardiogram was done and showed a highly mobile mass attached to the anterior mitral valve leaflet, measuring approximately 1.5 cm.
Due to the abnormal findings, he was transferred to our facility for further evaluation and management. On admission, the patient appeared well. Temperature was 36.8°C, blood pressure was 123/48, heart rate was 85 beats per minute, respiratory rate was 16 breaths per minute, and peripheral oxygen saturations were 98% on room air. A 1/6 holosystolic murmur heard best over the mitral valve was auscultated; the remainder of the physical examination was normal. There was no mention of onychomycosis. His laboratory evaluation demonstrated mild anemia (11.4 g/dL; reference 13.5–17.5 g/dL), elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (60 mm/1 hour; reference 0–22 mm/1 hour), and elevated C-reactive protein (75.9 mg/L; reference < 8.0 mg/L). The peripheral white blood cell count and differential, platelet count, INR, and prothrombin time were all normal.
The patient underwent transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) which revealed two highly mobile masses attached to the mitral valve with the largest mass measuring approximately 1 cm by 3 cm. The patient underwent surgery the following day. Intraoperatively, the mitral valve had firm, white vegetations with destruction of both anterior and posterior leaflets. Samples were taken and sent immediately to pathology for review. The preliminary frozen pathology demonstrated fungal elements (Figures and ). He underwent aggressive debridement and mitral valve replacement with a porcine valve. The patient was started on intravenous liposomal amphotericin B 5 mg/kg/day intraoperatively and caspofungin 50 mg daily was added the next day for empiric treatment while waiting identification of the organism. Final pathology described active native valve endocarditis with fungal organisms involving the myxomatous mitral valve. The mitral valve tissue was cultured on inhibitory mold agar and ultimately grew a Scopulariopsis species with no other microbial growth, including negative bacterial and mycobacterial cultures. Fungal blood cultures were obtained but were negative for growth. Due to the severity of the disease and highly resistant nature of the organism, oral voriconazole dosed at 300 mg twice daily was also added to the antifungal regimen.
The day following mitral valve debridement and replacement, vascular medicine and vascular surgery were consulted regarding the suspected mycotic aneurysm and thrombus of the left popliteal artery. CT angiogram with lower extremity runoff was repeated and showed the left popliteal artery mycotic aneurysm and thrombus, as well as bilateral renal infarcts. Ten days after mitral valve surgery, he underwent resection of the popliteal artery and peroneal artery bypass. The pathologic examination revealed GMS-positive staining hyphae, and inhibitory mold agar cultures again grew a pure culture of Scopulariopsis species.
The Scopulariopsis species from the cultures was ultimately identified by combined phenotypic characterization and DNA sequencing as Scopulariopsis brevicaulis. Phenotypically, the organism was a tan powdery colony and microscopically demonstrated branching annellides with chaining, round conidia with a truncated base consistent with Scopulariopsis brevicaulis. From a sequencing standpoint, D2 ribosomal sequencing was utilized with the top 3 matches being a greater than 95% match (100%, 99.31%, and 95.45%) to Scopulariopsis species in the Mayo Fungal Library. Antifungal susceptibilities were completed utilizing broth dilution as per recommended guidelines from the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute and were consistent with those reported in the literature: amphotericin 2 µg/mL, caspofungin 1 µg/mL, micafungin 0.125 µg/mL, anidulafungin 4 µg/mL, posaconazole 2 µg/mL, voriconazole 8 µg/mL, itraconazole 2 µg/mL, and terbinafine 0.5 µg/mL [].
Liposomal amphotericin was discontinued after 18 days due to acute renal injury, and caspofungin and oral voriconazole therapy were continued. He completed 8 weeks of combination therapy and was continued on oral voriconazole for chronic suppression. Voriconazole was chosen despite susceptibility data given reported clinical efficacy combined with lack of correlation between the susceptibility data and patient outcomes []. He unfortunately developed peripheral neuropathy, suspected by his local neurologist to be secondary to the voriconazole, and he was transitioned to delayed-release oral posaconazole dosed at 100 mg daily for chronic suppression [, ]. Posaconazole levels were followed and remained therapeutic with levels >1.0 mcg/mL. The inflammatory markers normalized at 6 months. Follow-up TEEs at 3 months and 7 months postoperatively did not show recurrence of endocarditis. Further, after cessation of the voriconazole he reported improvement, but not full resolution, of his neuropathy symptoms.

output.reasoning: The question explicitly requests the FeverPAIN score, which matches the available calculator.

step_info.thought:
The FeverPAIN score is calculated based on five criteria: Fever (>38°C), Purulence, Attend rapidly (<= 3 days), Inflamed tonsils, and No cough or coryza. According to the patient note, the patient had no fever (temperature 36.8°C), and there was no mention of tonsillar purulence, inflamed tonsils, or cough/coryza symptoms. Since the FeverPAIN criteria are designed for pharyngitis assessment and the patient presented for knee pain with no respiratory symptoms noted, he meets none of the criteria.

The FeverPAIN score is:
- Fever (>38°C): 0
- Purulence (tonsillar exudate): 0
- Attend rapidly (<=3 days): 0
- Inflamed tonsils: 0
- No cough/coryza: 0

Total Score = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0.

args: A 49-year-old woman is evaluated this morning in the regional block area prior to a scheduled elective right total knee replacement that the orthopaedic team anticipates will take approximately two hours under neuraxial anesthesia with a pneumatic tourniquet and primary cemented components; the plan is for immediate post-operative transfer to the joint-replacement ward and initiation of physical-therapy protocols tomorrow. She arrives on a stretcher, propped on pillows with the head of the bed elevated, and notes that progressive tricompartmental osteoarthritic pain over the past year has forced her to abandon her job as a catering supervisor and, more recently, to remain almost continuously recumbent because each attempt at weight bearing produces sharp medial knee grinding. Nursing flow sheets from the surgical admission that began yesterday document fewer than three assisted transfers to the bedside commode in a 24-hour period, and she states that at home during the preceding week she “basically lived between the mattress and the couch,” relying on her partner for meals, laundry, and bathing assistance. Her recent medical course is notable for having undergone a laparoscopic total hysterectomy with salpingo-oophorectomy 22 days ago for symptomatic leiomyomata; the operation was uncomplicated, but convalescence overlapped with a longstanding ankle problem, and two weeks ago the podiatry service performed an endoscopic debridement and Broström ligament reconstruction of the left ankle to address chronic instability. At the conclusion of that latter procedure a molded fiberglass below-knee cylinder was applied and remains in place today, extending from just below the fibular head to the metatarsal heads with the ankle in neutral; she has been instructed to keep the cast non-weight-bearing until her first post-operative clinic visit next week, and therefore propels herself with a knee scooter only for short indoor distances. She emphatically denies sustaining any fracture at the time of the ankle operation, and radiographs in the chart confirm intact bony architecture; the intervention was entirely soft-tissue. Over the past month she has not experienced fever, chills, night sweats, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, lower-lobe infiltrates, or any emergency evaluations for systemic infection, and the hysterectomy recovery was free of wound erythema or foul drainage. She has never carried a diagnosis of heart failure, emphysema, coronary disease, inflammatory bowel disease, malignancy, or clotting disorder, and she recounts no episodes of unilateral calf swelling, sudden dyspnea, or hemoptysis. No central venous catheter, PICC line, or implantable port has ever been placed; today’s only access is an 18-gauge peripheral IV in the right antecubital fossa. Maternal relatives have prominent leg veins, and she herself displays diffuse serpiginous bluish tributaries coursing along the posteromedial aspect of both calves that become more apparent when her feet dangle off the stretcher, though the skin is warm, supple, and free of pitting or focal tenderness. She denies prior heparin reactions, and outpatient laboratory data never showed platelet suppression. Medications this morning include acetaminophen for pain, vitamin D 2000 IU, and an over-the-counter glucosamine supplement; she takes no estrogen products, selective estrogen receptor modulators, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or chronic steroids. She never smoked, consumes wine socially once a month, and denies illicit drug use. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, palpitations, syncope, headaches, visual changes, abdominal pain, diarrhea, melena, or neurologic deficit. On examination she rests comfortably, conversing in full sentences without distress. Vital signs: temperature 36.6 °C, blood pressure 124/72 mm Hg, heart rate 76/min regular, respiratory rate 14/min, oxygen saturation 97 % on room air; body mass index is 29 kg/m². Conjunctivae are pink, sclerae clear, mucous membranes moist. Neck veins are not distended, carotid pulses are brisk, and the precordial examination reveals a nondisplaced point of maximal impulse with crisp S₁ and S₂ and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear to percussion and auscultation with symmetric chest expansion. Abdomen is flat with healed port-site scars in the periumbilical and suprapubic regions; bowel sounds are present, and there is no guarding or rebound. The right knee is valgus aligned, moderately enlarged, and tender along the medial joint line with crepitus on passive flexion beyond 90°, but no effusion or erythema. The left lower leg is enclosed in the intact fiberglass cast described above; capillary refill at exposed toes is brisk, pedal pulses palpate 2+, and the skin around the cast edge is intact. Both distal calves measure 35 cm in circumference with no asymmetry. Ropy subcutaneous veins course along the posterior calf bilaterally without inflammation or induration. Neurologic screening demonstrates alert mentation, intact cranial nerves II–XII, 5/5 strength in the right lower limb proximally and distally, intact sensation to light touch and pinprick, and physiologic deep tendon reflexes. Laboratory studies drawn at 5 a.m. show hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, hematocrit 39 %, platelet count 246 × 10⁹/L, white blood cells 6.8 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 0.84 mg/dL, AST 21 U/L, ALT 18 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, prothrombin time 12.0 s with an international normalized ratio of 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 29 s. A 12-lead electrocardiogram recorded on admission demonstrates normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm with no ST-T abnormalities, and a portable chest radiograph is clear. Airway assessment reveals Mallampati class II, full cervical extension, adequate inter-incisor distance, and no dental instability. She is nil per os since midnight, voided recently without difficulty, sequential compression sleeves are positioned at the bedside awaiting application in the operating theatre, and no antithrombotic medication has been administered yet pending completion of intraoperative risk assessment.
What is the patient's Caprini Score for Venous Thromboembolism? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.
