args: A 50-year-old man with no prior thromboembolic events, no recent hospitalizations, and no surgery—minor or major—performed within the past 12 weeks walked into the ambulatory triage area this morning because he noticed that from groin to ankle his left leg had become noticeably larger, heavy, and mildly painful over the preceding 18 hours. He works as a delivery van driver and completed an 11-hour interstate route two days ago, punctuated by brief loading stops but otherwise seated; since returning he has resumed his usual routine of evening dog walks and has not spent any full day in bed, worn a splint, or used crutches, casts, or braces, and he retains full motor control of both lower limbs. Yesterday afternoon he first appreciated a deep ache high in the medial thigh when stepping out of his truck, attributing it to muscle strain, yet overnight the discomfort intensified and by dawn the entire limb felt tight inside his jeans while the opposite side fit normally, prompting today’s visit. He denies trauma, falls, insect bites, fever, rigors, sweats, skin breaks, rash, groin mass, flank pain, dysuria, abdominal tenderness, chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, cough, dyspnea, palpitations, presyncope, weight loss, or neurologic deficit. Medications are limited to over-the-counter ibuprofen taken once or twice monthly for tension headache; he uses no hormonal therapy, steroids, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or anticoagulants. Family history is negative for venous or arterial clots. He has never smoked, drinks two beers on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. On arrival—before receiving any analgesic, intravenous fluid, or antithrombotic—he sat upright with the left leg elevated on a pillow. Triage vitals were: temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 126/82 mm Hg, heart rate 94 beats min⁻¹ regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths min⁻¹, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Cardiac examination revealed a crisp S₁ and S₂ without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; jugular venous pressure was not distended. Lungs were clear to auscultation throughout. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, and nontender, with no hepatosplenomegaly or ascites. Lower-extremity inspection showed conspicuous asymmetry: from the inguinal crease to the malleoli the entire left limb was circumferentially enlarged and the overlying skin appeared stretched yet maintained normal coloration and temperature. Flexible tape placed 15 cm above the superior border of the patella measured 56.2 cm on the left versus 50.8 cm on the right; at exactly 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity circumferences were 44.0 cm left and 39.3 cm right, documenting a 4.7 cm calf disparity. Around the medial malleolus the values were 30.1 cm and 26.4 cm respectively. With the limb dependent for 30 seconds no finger indentation persisted after direct pressure over the anterior tibial crest, and the ankle did not demonstrate pits, suggesting the swelling was non-pitting. When the examiner traced the anticipated course of the common femoral vein under the inguinal ligament, along the adductor canal, and into the popliteal fossa, firm fingertip pressure reproduced a sharply localized tenderness most pronounced midway down the medial thigh; identical pressure over adjacent quadriceps or hamstring bellies elicited only diffuse pressure sensation. No palpable cord, crepitus, fluctuance, erythema, brawny discoloration, or cystic swelling was detected. Passive ankle dorsiflexion with the knee extended provoked only a stretching sensation, not sudden pain. Hip, knee, and ankle ranges of motion were full and symmetric; distal strength graded 5/5, sensation to light touch and vibration was intact, and dorsalis pedis as well as posterior tibial pulses were 2+ bilaterally with capillary refill <2 s. No inguinal or popliteal lymphadenopathy was appreciated. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained prior to phlebotomy showed normal sinus rhythm at 92 beats min⁻¹ without ST-T wave changes. Laboratory studies drawn before any therapy revealed hemoglobin 14.1 g dL⁻¹, platelet count 236 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, leukocytes 7.0 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, sodium 138 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 4.1 mmol L⁻¹, bicarbonate 23 mmol L⁻¹, blood urea nitrogen 12 mg dL⁻¹, creatinine 0.90 mg dL⁻¹, AST 19 U L⁻¹, ALT 18 U L⁻¹, international normalized ratio 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 27 s. Upright anteroposterior chest radiograph displayed clear lung fields and normal cardiac silhouette; a plain radiograph of the femur and tibia requested by his occupational clinic earlier in the day showed intact cortices with only soft-tissue prominence. No cellulitis, Baker cyst rupture, musculoskeletal tear, lymphatic obstruction, heart failure, renal disease, or systemic volume overload was identified that would more convincingly account for an acute full-limb enlargement accompanied by focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway and a measured calf circumference difference greater than 3 cm, so duplex ultrasonography from the iliac confluence through the infrapopliteal veins was arranged while the patient remained hemodynamically stable, unmedicated, and with the leg elevated pending imaging.

step_info.args: {"patient_note": "A 50-year-old man with no prior thromboembolic events, no recent hospitalizations, and no surgery—minor or major—performed within the past 12 weeks walked into the ambulatory triage area this morning because he noticed that from groin to ankle his left leg had become noticeably larger, heavy, and mildly painful over the preceding 18 hours. He works as a delivery van driver and completed an 11-hour interstate route two days ago, punctuated by brief loading stops but otherwise seated; since returning he has resumed his usual routine of evening dog walks and has not spent any full day in bed, worn a splint, or used crutches, casts, or braces, and he retains full motor control of both lower limbs. Yesterday afternoon he first appreciated a deep ache high in the medial thigh when stepping out of his truck, attributing it to muscle strain, yet overnight the discomfort intensified and by dawn the entire limb felt tight inside his jeans while the opposite side fit normally, prompting today’s visit. He denies trauma, falls, insect bites, fever, rigors, sweats, skin breaks, rash, groin mass, flank pain, dysuria, abdominal tenderness, chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, cough, dyspnea, palpitations, presyncope, weight loss, or neurologic deficit. Medications are limited to over-the-counter ibuprofen taken once or twice monthly for tension headache; he uses no hormonal therapy, steroids, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or anticoagulants. Family history is negative for venous or arterial clots. He has never smoked, drinks two beers on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. On arrival—before receiving any analgesic, intravenous fluid, or antithrombotic—he sat upright with the left leg elevated on a pillow. Triage vitals were: temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 126/82 mm Hg, heart rate 94 beats min⁻¹ regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths min⁻¹, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Cardiac examination revealed a crisp S₁ and S₂ without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; jugular venous pressure was not distended. Lungs were clear to auscultation throughout. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, and nontender, with no hepatosplenomegaly or ascites. Lower-extremity inspection showed conspicuous asymmetry: from the inguinal crease to the malleoli the entire left limb was circumferentially enlarged and the overlying skin appeared stretched yet maintained normal coloration and temperature. Flexible tape placed 15 cm above the superior border of the patella measured 56.2 cm on the left versus 50.8 cm on the right; at exactly 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity circumferences were 44.0 cm left and 39.3 cm right, documenting a 4.7 cm calf disparity. Around the medial malleolus the values were 30.1 cm and 26.4 cm respectively. With the limb dependent for 30 seconds no finger indentation persisted after direct pressure over the anterior tibial crest, and the ankle did not demonstrate pits, suggesting the swelling was non-pitting. When the examiner traced the anticipated course of the common femoral vein under the inguinal ligament, along the adductor canal, and into the popliteal fossa, firm fingertip pressure reproduced a sharply localized tenderness most pronounced midway down the medial thigh; identical pressure over adjacent quadriceps or hamstring bellies elicited only diffuse pressure sensation. No palpable cord, crepitus, fluctuance, erythema, brawny discoloration, or cystic swelling was detected. Passive ankle dorsiflexion with the knee extended provoked only a stretching sensation, not sudden pain. Hip, knee, and ankle ranges of motion were full and symmetric; distal strength graded 5/5, sensation to light touch and vibration was intact, and dorsalis pedis as well as posterior tibial pulses were 2+ bilaterally with capillary refill <2 s. No inguinal or popliteal lymphadenopathy was appreciated. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained prior to phlebotomy showed normal sinus rhythm at 92 beats min⁻¹ without ST-T wave changes. Laboratory studies drawn before any therapy revealed hemoglobin 14.1 g dL⁻¹, platelet count 236 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, leukocytes 7.0 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, sodium 138 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 4.1 mmol L⁻¹, bicarbonate 23 mmol L⁻¹, blood urea nitrogen 12 mg dL⁻¹, creatinine 0.90 mg dL⁻¹, AST 19 U L⁻¹, ALT 18 U L⁻¹, international normalized ratio 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 27 s. Upright anteroposterior chest radiograph displayed clear lung fields and normal cardiac silhouette; a plain radiograph of the femur and tibia requested by his occupational clinic earlier in the day showed intact cortices with only soft-tissue prominence. No cellulitis, Baker cyst rupture, musculoskeletal tear, lymphatic obstruction, heart failure, renal disease, or systemic volume overload was identified that would more convincingly account for an acute full-limb enlargement accompanied by focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway and a measured calf disparity greater than 3 cm, so duplex ultrasonography from the iliac confluence through the infrapopliteal veins was arranged while the patient remained hemodynamically stable, unmedicated, and with the leg elevated pending imaging.", "required_values": ["age", "sex"]}

step_info.thought:
The Wells' Criteria for DVT is a validated clinical decision tool used to estimate the pre-test probability of a deep vein thrombosis. Based on the patient note, the following points are assigned:
- Entire leg swollen: +1 point
- Calf swelling > 3 cm: +1 point
- Localized tenderness along the deep venous system: +1 point
- All other criteria (previous DVT, active cancer, bedridden/surgery, paralysis/cast, collateral superficial veins, pitting edema, and alternative diagnosis more likely) are absent (0 points).
- The sum of these findings results in a score of 3.0.

calculator_name: Revised Cardiac Risk Index for Pre-Operative Risk

args: A 74-year-old woman was referred to the peri-operative medicine service nine days before a planned open pancreaticoduodenectomy for a 2.5-cm head-of-pancreas adenocarcinoma discovered on a contrast CT that also demonstrated double-duct sign and no radiographic evidence of vascular encasement; the hepatobiliary team anticipates a midline laparotomy with operative time approaching six hours, possible need for portal vein reconstruction, and postoperative management in the surgical intensive care unit for hemodynamic monitoring. She arrived by taxi, ambulated the 60-meter clinic corridor without assistance, and stated that her usual activities include preparing meals, tending a small backyard herb garden, and climbing the twelve steps to her bedroom several times daily, though she prefers to pause on the landing to catch her breath when carrying a laundry basket; she attributes this limitation to deconditioning after two recent bouts of cholangitis that were treated with endoscopic stenting and antibiotics. Eight years ago she experienced an abrupt episode of left-hand clumsiness and slurred speech while reading, prompting emergency evaluation; diffusion-weighted brain MRI confirmed a small right corona radiata infarct, and symptoms resolved completely over 48 hours. She has had no subsequent focal neurologic events, visual disturbances, or gait imbalance, attends weekly bridge club without cognitive difficulty, and remains adherent to aspirin 81 mg nightly and high-intensity atorvastatin initiated at hospital discharge. She denies ever feeling substernal pressure, arm or jaw discomfort, exertional tightness, or palpitations, has never undergone coronary angiography, stress imaging, or cardiac catheter-based intervention, and her primary physician has not documented abnormal electrocardiograms beyond age-appropriate ST-T changes. Medical history is otherwise notable for well-controlled primary hypertension diagnosed in her sixties managed with amlodipine 5 mg each morning and losartan 50 mg daily, stage 2 chronic kidney disease attributed to hypertensive nephrosclerosis, and prediabetes recognized during last year’s wellness visit with hemoglobin A1c of 6.1 % for which she follows a carbohydrate-restricted diet and performs 30 minutes of stationary cycling three times weekly; she has never been prescribed injectable or oral glucose-lowering medication. There is no diagnosis of peripheral arterial disease, chronic lung condition, venous thromboembolism, or peptic ulcer. Surgical history includes an uncomplicated laparoscopic hysterectomy for fibroids at age 52 and cataract extraction of the right eye two years ago. She quit cigarette smoking 30 years ago after a 12-pack-year exposure, drinks a glass of white wine with Sunday dinner, and denies illicit drug use. Her mother died of an intracerebral hemorrhage at 79, and her father succumbed to colorectal cancer at 70; no first-degree relatives have documented coronary disease before age 65. Review of systems today is negative for fevers, night sweats, hemoptysis, paroxysmal coughing, melena, or unintended weight change beyond the 6 lb loss since the diagnosis attributable to early satiety. After five minutes seated, vital signs are temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 128/72 mm Hg in the right arm, regular pulse 72 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 97 % on ambient air; body mass index is 27 kg/m². Jugular venous pulsation is not elevated, carotid upstrokes are brisk without bruit, and the point of maximal impulse lies at the fifth intercostal space in the mid-clavicular line. Cardiac auscultation reveals a crisp S1 and S2 with no additional sounds or murmurs. Breath sounds are vesicular to the bases without crackles, wheezes, or pleural rub. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness over the stent site, no rebound or guarding, and palpable but non-tender hepatomegaly 2 cm below the costal margin. Radial, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ and symmetric, and there is no peripheral edema or calf tenderness. Neurologic examination shows alert mentation, intact cranial nerves, fluent speech, full motor strength, normal sensation, symmetric reflexes, and a steady tandem gait. Pre-operative laboratory studies drawn one hour before the visit reveal hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, hematocrit 38 %, platelet count 245 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.8 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 21 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.22 mg/dL, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 54 mL/min/1.73 m²; total bilirubin is 1.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 168 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 42 U/L, international normalized ratio 1.1, and fasting plasma glucose 108 mg/dL. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained today demonstrates normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats per minute, normal PR and QRS intervals, and nonspecific T-wave flattening unchanged from a tracing six months prior. Upright posterior-anterior chest radiograph displays clear lung fields and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. Airway assessment by anesthesia shows Mallampati class II visualization, inter-incisor distance 4 cm, thyromental distance 6 cm, and full cervical extension. The anesthesiology team plans endotracheal general anesthesia with arterial line placement and epidural analgesia for postoperative pain control, will continue antihypertensive medications the morning of surgery, target euvolemia with balanced crystalloid, administer stress-dose hydrocortisone only if refractory hypotension occurs, and arrange hourly serum glucose measurements with dextrose-containing maintenance fluids adjusted as needed. The surgical team has reviewed potential need for vascular graft, pancreatic leak, delayed gastric emptying, and peri-operative neurologic or cardiac complications; the patient demonstrated comprehension, voiced specific concern about potential prolonged recovery due to her prior stroke, and expressed determination to proceed in hopes of curative resection.

What is the patient's score of the Revised Cardiac Risk Index for Pre-Operative Risk? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args: {"patient_note": "A 74-year-old woman was referred to the peri-operative medicine service nine days before a planned open pancreaticoduodenectomy... history... stage 2 chronic kidney disease... hypertension... eight years ago she experienced an abrupt episode of left-hand clumsiness and slurred speech... MRI confirmed a small right corona radiata infarct... creatinine 1.22 mg/dL... hemodynamics... surgery is a planned open pancreaticoduodenectomy.", "required_values": ["age", "sex", "creatinine_mg_dl", "history_of_ischemic_heart_disease", "history_of_congestive_heart_failure", "history_of_cerebrovascular_disease", "history_of_diabetes_mellitus", "history_of_chronic_kidney_disease", "type_of_surgery"]}

step_info.thought:
The RCRI (Revised Cardiac Risk Index) assesses six factors: 1) High-risk surgery (intraperitoneal, intrathoracic, or suprainguinal vascular surgery), 2) History of ischemic heart disease, 3) History of heart failure, 4) History of cerebrovascular disease, 5) Preoperative treatment with insulin (for diabetes), and 6) Preoperative serum creatinine > 2.0 mg/dL. 

From the note:
- High-risk surgery: Yes (open pancreaticoduodenectomy is an intraperitoneal procedure).
- History of ischemic heart disease: No (denies symptoms, no cardiac history).
- History of heart failure: No.
- History of cerebrovascular disease: Yes (prior stroke).
- Diabetes mellitus: The note says she has prediabetes (A1c 6.1%), never prescribed insulin, so this criterion is not met.
- Preoperative serum creatinine > 2.0 mg/dL: No (it is 1.22 mg/dL).

The criteria met are: 1) High-risk surgery, 2) History of cerebrovascular disease. This gives a total score of 2.

args: A 58-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cirrhosis attributed to long-standing non-alcoholic steatohepatitis presented to the tertiary-care emergency department late in the evening because coworkers had noticed during the past forty-eight hours that she required gentle prompting to remember the password for the shipping manifest, paused briefly while counting out coins, and at one point addressed Wednesday’s documents with Thursday’s date; she herself was concerned that earlier that morning she missed an exit on a familiar commute and felt “foggy” when balancing her checkbook. She reported no frank disorientation, had driven safely with her spouse in the passenger seat, and continued to manage basic activities of daily living, although she admitted that her handwriting had become slightly untidy over the prior week. The patient denied vomiting, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, fever, chills, chest pain, dyspnea, or new dermatologic lesions. For the past month she had noticed her waistband felt snug by bedtime and that rolling onto her flank in bed occasionally produced a subtle sloshing sensation across the lower abdomen, but she could still button her jeans and denied marked distention, early satiety, orthopnea, or pedal swelling. Medical history was notable for hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily, class II obesity after a 30-pound weight loss program, and remote laparoscopic appendectomy; there had been no prior episodes of gastrointestinal bleeding, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, or paracentesis, and she was not taking lactulose, rifaximin, diuretics, non-selective beta-blockers, or vitamin K. She never smoked, drinks wine only at weddings, and has never used recreational drugs. There is no family history of inherited liver disease; her father died of stroke at 76 and her mother has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes. Review of systems was otherwise negative for pruritus, jaundice noticed by acquaintances, bruising, muscle cramps, or unintended weight change. On arrival—before intravenous access, fluids, or medications—vital signs were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/68 mm Hg, heart rate 86 beats/min, respiratory rate 15 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. She sat upright, maintained eye contact, and answered most questions appropriately but delayed three seconds naming the current month and guessed the exact date off by one day; when asked to extend her arms and dorsiflex the wrists, a low-amplitude flap appeared after ten seconds. Speech was fluent without dysarthria, cranial nerves were intact, motor strength remained 5/5 symmetrically, reflexes were physiologic, sensation was preserved, and her gait down the hallway was narrow-based though slightly slow. Breath had a faint sweet odor. Sclerae displayed mild icterus, conjunctivae were pale-pink, and fewer than ten spider angiomas dotted the upper chest. Jugular venous pulsation measured 7 cm H₂O at 30°. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rhythm with normal S1 and S2, without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear. The abdominal wall was mildly rounded with the umbilicus still concave; percussion produced central tympany transitioning to a slim band of dullness along the dependent flank that shifted when she rolled, and a transmitted impulse across the abdomen required the examiner’s palpating hand to feel a modest wave despite counter-pressure. The liver edge was firm and nodular, palpable 1 cm beneath the right costal margin on deep inspiration, non-tender, and the spleen tip was not felt. No ankle edema or sacral edema was observed, and muscle bulk over the temporal and quadriceps regions was preserved. Capillary glucose measured 103 mg/dL. Point-of-care ultrasound obtained prior to phlebotomy demonstrated a shrunken heterogeneous liver with capsular irregularity, patent main portal vein with antegrade flow at 14 cm/s, a spleen length of 15 cm, and a thin anechoic stripe of fluid less than 2 cm in the right paracolic gutter; kidneys were normal in size and echogenicity, and there was no biliary dilation or focal hepatic mass. Blood drawn immediately afterward—before any vitamin K, albumin, cathartics, or antibiotics—revealed total bilirubin 2.3 mg/dL, serum albumin 3.65 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 62 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 79 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 154 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 198 U/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 21 mg/dL, creatinine 0.98 mg/dL (eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73 m²), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, white blood cell count 5.9 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 102 × 10⁹/L, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.74. Serum ammonia, hepatitis serologies, and alpha-fetoprotein were sent and remain pending. Upright chest radiograph obtained before any therapies showed elevated diaphragms without effusion or infiltrate, and a twelve-lead electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm at 84 beats/min with a QTc of 432 ms. At this juncture no intravenous fluids, laxatives, diuretics, albumin, benzodiazepines, or blood products had been administered, ensuring that all clinical observations and laboratory values represent her untreated physiologic state at presentation.
What is the patient's Child-Pugh Score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

She reported no frank disorientation... when asked to extend her arms and dorsiflex the wrists, a low-amplitude flap appeared after ten seconds."}

step_info.thought:
The patient's values extracted from the clinical note are: Total bilirubin 2.3 mg/dL, serum albumin 3.65 g/dL, INR 1.74, ascites categorized as mild, and hepatic encephalopathy grade 1. I need to calculate the Child-Pugh score based on these findings by assigning points to each category.

*   **Total Bilirubin (2.3 mg/dL):** 2.0-3.0 range = 2 points
*   **Serum Albumin (3.65 g/dL):** 2.8-3.5 range = 2 points (Note: Often <2.8 is 3, 2.8-3.5 is 2, >3.5 is 1 point; since 3.65 > 3.5, this is 1 point)
*   **INR (1.74):** 1.7-2.3 range = 2 points
*   **Ascites (Mild):** 1 point
*   **Hepatic Encephalopathy (Grade 1):** 1 point

Total Score = 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 7.

Wait, let me re-verify the standard Child-Pugh scoring ranges:
- Bilirubin: <2 (1), 2-3 (2), >3 (3). Score: 2
- Albumin: >3.5 (1), 2.8-3.5 (2), <2.8 (3). Score: 1
- INR: <1.7 (1), 1.7-2.3 (2), >2.3 (3). Score: 2
- Ascites: None (1), Mild (2), Moderate/Severe (3). Score: 2
- Encephalopathy: None (1), Grade 1-2 (2), Grade 3-4 (3). Score: 2

Let's re-read the note for ascites and encephalopathy. The note mentions a "slim band of dullness... that shifted" (mild ascites) and "gentle prompting" / "low-amplitude flap" (Grade 1 encephalopathy).

Re-calculating:
- Bilirubin 2.3: 2 points
- Albumin 3.65: 1 point
- INR 1.74: 2 points
- Ascites (Mild): 2 points
- Encephalopathy (Grade 1): 2 points
Total: 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 9.

Wait, let me double check the standard ranges again.
Child-Pugh points:
Bilirubin (mg/dL): <2 (1), 2-3 (2), >3 (3). (2.3 is 2 pts)
Albumin (g/dL): >3.5 (1), 2.8-3.5 (2), <2.8 (3). (3.65 is 1 pt)
INR: <1.7 (1), 1.7-2.3 (2), >2.3 (3). (1.74 is 2 pts)
Ascites: None (1), Mild (2), Moderate/Severe (3). (Mild is 2 pts)
Encephalopathy: None (1), Grade 1-2 (2), Grade 3-4 (3). (Grade 1 is 2 pts)

Sum = 2+1+2+2+2 = 9.

One final check on the ranges:
Some sources use Albumin >3.5 = 1, 2.8-3.5 = 2, <2.8 = 3. Correct.
Some sources use INR <1.7 = 1, 1.7-2.3 = 2, >2.3 = 3. Correct.
Some sources use Ascites: None=1, Mild=2, Moderate=3. Correct.
Some sources use Encephalopathy: None=1, Grade 1-2 = 2, Grade 3-4 = 3. Correct.

The score is 9.

args:
A 43-year-old Caucasian woman is admitted to the hospital with acute onset right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain. The pain started 6 hours ago after the patient had a large meal at a birthday party and has progressively worsened. She recalls having similar pain before but not so intense. No significant past medical history. Current medications are only oral contraceptive. Vitals are blood pressure 140/80 mm Hg, heart rate 79/min, respiratory rate 14/min, and temperature 37.6℃ (99.7℉). The patient’s BMI is 36.3 kg/m2. On exam, the patient appears slightly jaundiced. Her cardiac and respiratory examinations are within normal limits. Abdominal palpation reveals tenderness to palpation in the RUQ with no rebound or guarding, and there is an inspiratory arrest on deep palpation in this region. The remainder of the examination is within normal limits.
Laboratory tests are significant for the following:
RBC count 4.1 million/mm3
Hb 13.4 mg/dL
Leukocyte count 11,200/mm3
ESR 22 mm/hr
Platelet count 230,000/mm3
Total bilirubin 2 mg/dL
Direct bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL
ALT 20 IU/L
AST 18 IU/L
Amylase 33 IU/L
Ultrasound of the abdomen shows the following result (see image):
The common bile duct (CBD) (not shown in the image) is not dilated.

args: A 47-year-old man receiving his fifth cycle of infusional FOLFOX-bevacizumab for metastatic sigmoid adenocarcinoma arrived to the medical day hospital late this morning because the lower half of his left leg had grown noticeably fuller and achy since the previous evening. He drives himself to treatments and continues to work part-time as an IT consultant, typically seated at a desk for two-hour stretches before getting up to refill coffee or walk about the office; over the past week he had logged roughly 6,000–7,000 steps daily on his fitness watch, had completed a 30-minute neighborhood walk with his partner the night before the swelling began, and has not spent more than a single day confined to bed at any point during chemotherapy. He has not experienced fevers, rigors, night sweats, rash, insect bite, recent travel, prolonged flights, or trauma to the limb, and he flatly denies any prior clot in his legs or lungs. His only surgeries were a laparoscopic low anterior resection and port placement eight months ago at the time of diagnosis, both wounds now well healed; he has had no additional operations, endoscopic procedures, catheterizations, orthopedic casts, or splints within the past twelve weeks, and there have been no episodes of limb weakness or numbness since. Other medical history includes well-controlled dyspepsia on pantoprazole and mild hypertension treated with lisinopril 10 mg daily. He has never taken estrogen, testosterone, or anabolic supplements, does not smoke, drinks an occasional craft beer on weekends, and uses no recreational drugs. There is no family history of venous thromboembolism, connective-tissue disease, or early coronary events. On review of systems he notes a dull pressure that intensifies when he points the toes upward or when the examiner presses behind the knee, but no redness, pruritus, weeping skin, ankle discoloration, foot numbness, or back pain. Upon arrival—before any medications or intravenous fluids were started—vital signs read temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/76 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats/min and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. He walked to the examination cot without assistance, though favoring the left side. Cardiac auscultation revealed a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs or gallops; lungs were clear bilaterally. Abdomen was soft with a well-healed periumbilical port scar and no hepatosplenomegaly. Inspection of the lower limbs showed a pronounced increase in girth limited to the mid-calf region on the left: at a standardized landmark 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity the tape measured 43.8 cm on the symptomatic side versus 39.5 cm on the right, a disparity of 4.3 cm. Thigh circumference 15 cm above the patella was symmetric at 50 cm, and ankle measurement around the malleoli differed by less than 1 cm. The overlying skin retained normal coloration without erythema, streaking, induration, or visible straight or lattice-like subcutaneous venous channels, and no distended collateral veins were seen. Palpation along the course of the popliteal and posterior tibial vessels elicited a discrete, reproducible soreness, whereas compressing the gastrocnemius belly laterally produced only mild, diffuse discomfort; no subcutaneous cords or nodules were appreciated. The limb was neither unusually warm nor cool compared with the contralateral side when tested with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia left no persistent indentation. Passive ankle dorsiflexion with the knee extended provoked a tugging ache deep in the calf, while hip and knee ranges of motion were full and painless. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2 + bilaterally, capillary refill in all toes was under two seconds, and motor strength of plantarflexion and dorsiflexion graded 5/5 without sensory deficits. No inguinal or popliteal adenopathy was detected. Baseline laboratory results drawn before any anticoagulation showed hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, platelet count 312 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.8 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.86 mg/dL, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, alanine aminotransferase 21 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 24 U/L, international normalized ratio 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 29 seconds. A point-of-care urinalysis was unremarkable and electrocardiography showed normal sinus rhythm. Given the acute unilateral calf enlargement exceeding three centimeters, the pinpoint tenderness traced precisely over the deep venous path, the ongoing malignancy known to confer hypercoagulability, the absence of immobilization, recent surgery, or prior clotting episodes, and the lack of dermatologic or musculoskeletal findings that would favor cellulitis, Achilles rupture, or compartment syndrome, no competing diagnosis currently rivals an acute deep venous thrombosis in likelihood, so cross-sectional vascular imaging of the entire left lower extremity has been requested urgently while oncology infusion is temporarily held.

What is the patient's score of Wells' criteria for Deep Vein Thrombosis? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.thought: The patient's clinical presentation, particularly the metastatic sigmoid adenocarcinoma, implies active malignancy. He also presents with unilateral calf swelling of 4.3 cm (exceeding the 3 cm threshold) and localized tenderness along the deep venous system. According to the Wells' Criteria for DVT, the criteria met are: active cancer (+1), calf swelling >3cm (+1), and localized tenderness (+1). All other criteria, such as prior DVT, paralysis/plaster, immobilization, or alternative diagnosis at least as likely, are not met. Therefore, the score is 3.
